statement.description: The problem statement in plain prose.

constraints.description: Input sizes / value ranges that determine which complexities pass.

approach.description: Prose description of the naive correct solution.
Prose description of the optimal solution.

language.description: Language of the code field, e.g. python.

code.description: A complete, runnable reference solution. Used for verification; the student NEVER sees this.

key_insight.description: The single 'aha' that unlocks the optimal solution, stated plainly.

ladder.description: The problem-specific rungs from naive to optimal, as short phrases. A map for the teacher, not a script.

counterexample.description: A concrete input that exposes the flaw, if applicable.

leak_terms.description: Terms/short phrases that would give away the KEY INSIGHT or the specific technique/data-structure. Specific destination-revealing words only (e.g. 'hash map', 'remember seen values for O(1) lookup'). NEVER generic programming vocabulary (e.g. 'array', 'loop', 'variable').

underlying_primitive.description: The core CS concept the student must possess, phrased for picking an analogy if they lack it, e.g. 'O(1) membership/lookup by key'.

input.description: A call to the reference solution's entrypoint, e.g. 'two_sum([2,7,11,15], 9)'.

output.description: The expected return value as a Python literal, e.g. '[0, 1]'.

examples.description: Worked examples used to VERIFY the reference code before the card is trusted.
